title: Patch a SharePoint people picker field from Power Apps
shortDescription: Save a selected person from a combo box into a SharePoint person column.
fullDescription: A reliable pattern for patching single-value SharePoint person fields from Power Apps using the claims object SharePoint expects.
problemStatement: Power Apps person values do not save correctly when the app sends only a display name or email address to SharePoint.
whenToUse: Combo boxes connected to Microsoft 365 users
whenNotToUse: Multi-person fields without adapting the table shape
Dataverse user lookups
stepByStepInstructions: Confirm the SharePoint column allows one person.
Use a combo box that returns Mail and DisplayName.
Patch the full expanded user object, including Claims.
Refresh the record or collection after save.
commonMistakes: Patching only the email string.
Using UserPrincipalName when the combo box returns Mail.
Forgetting to lower-case the claim email in tenants with mixed casing.
troubleshooting: If the field saves blank, inspect the combo box selected record shape.
If SharePoint rejects the value, confirm the column is not multi-select.

title: Patch a SharePoint choice field
shortDescription: Write choice values from Power Apps to SharePoint without schema errors.
fullDescription: Use the expected Value object when saving a single-choice SharePoint column from a dropdown or combo box.
problemStatement: Choice fields often fail when the app patches plain text instead of a SharePoint choice object.
whenNotToUse: Multi-choice fields without using a table of values
Columns stored as plain text
stepByStepInstructions: Bind the dropdown to Choices(Requests.Status).
Patch the selected value inside a Value object.
Refresh the item after saving if the screen depends on the new value.
commonMistakes: Using ddStatus.SelectedText.Value in modern controls.
Patching the whole selected record when only Value is expected.
troubleshooting: If the formula errors, check whether the control returns Result, Value, or a custom field name.

title: Patch a SharePoint lookup field
shortDescription: Save a lookup selection with the Id and Value shape SharePoint expects.
fullDescription: A lookup patch pattern for connecting related SharePoint list records from a Power Apps form.
problemStatement: Lookup columns need both an Id and display Value; sending plain text will not connect the related record.
whenNotToUse: Large lookup lists where delegation needs special handling
Dataverse relationships
stepByStepInstructions: Load the lookup source into the combo box.
Confirm the selected record includes ID and Title.
Patch Id and Value together.
Test with a new item and an edit item.
commonMistakes: Using lowercase id instead of ID from SharePoint.
Patching the lookup display text only.
troubleshooting: If the lookup shows blank after save, verify the source item still exists and the ID is correct.

title: Save gallery rows as JSON in a multiline text field
shortDescription: Store a lightweight editable table from Power Apps in one SharePoint item.
fullDescription: Use JSON serialization when a small child table does not justify a separate SharePoint list yet.
problemStatement: Small repeating sections can be cumbersome to save when the business wants one parent SharePoint item.
whenNotToUse: Rows that need permissions, workflows, or reporting
Large datasets
Child records with independent lifecycles
stepByStepInstructions: Keep the collection schema stable.
Serialize the collection with JSONFormat.Compact.
Save to a plain multiline text column.
Use ParseJSON to rehydrate the collection when editing.
commonMistakes: Using this pattern for data that should be relational.
Letting control-only columns leak into saved JSON.
troubleshooting: If the JSON is too large, move child rows to a dedicated SharePoint list.

title: Parse JSON from SharePoint back into a Power Apps collection
shortDescription: Rehydrate saved JSON rows into an editable collection.
fullDescription: Convert a multiline JSON field back into a typed Power Apps collection so users can continue editing rows.
problemStatement: ParseJSON returns untyped values, so apps need explicit conversion before collection rows behave predictably.
whenToUse: Loading draft checklist or deliverable rows
whenNotToUse: True relational child data
Large datasets
stepByStepInstructions: Check that the saved field is not blank.
Parse the JSON text.
Map each property into the expected type.
Bind the gallery to the rebuilt collection.
commonMistakes: Skipping Text(), Boolean(), or Value() conversions.
Assuming ParseJSON returns a typed table.
troubleshooting: If rows load with blank fields, compare the saved JSON property names to the parsing formula.

title: Submit multiple Power Apps forms together
shortDescription: Coordinate several edit forms and keep the user experience predictable.
fullDescription: A practical structure for validating and submitting multiple forms that write to related data sources.
problemStatement: Multiple forms can submit out of order or leave partial saves when validation and error handling are scattered.
whenToUse: Long request forms split into sections
whenNotToUse: Simple single-list forms
Transactions requiring guaranteed rollback
stepByStepInstructions: Validate all forms before submitting.
Use OnSuccess to chain dependent saves if one form needs the first record ID.
Use OnFailure to show clear feedback.
Disable the submit button while saving.
commonMistakes: Submitting child forms before the parent item exists.
Only checking one form's Valid property.
troubleshooting: If one form saves and another fails, move dependent saves into OnSuccess and add clear recovery messaging.

title: Refresh selectedRequest after Patch
shortDescription: Keep the current record fresh after saving changes.
fullDescription: Refresh and reload the selected SharePoint item so calculated fields, status values, and related UI update correctly.
problemStatement: After Patch, the screen can keep showing old values because the selected variable was not updated.
whenNotToUse: Purely local collection-only screens
stepByStepInstructions: Store the Patch result.
Refresh the data source when SharePoint-calculated values matter.
Look up the saved item again by ID.
Rebind detail controls to the selected variable.
commonMistakes: Refreshing without updating the selected variable.
Looking up by title instead of ID.
troubleshooting: If the UI still shows old data, confirm controls reference selectedRequest rather than Gallery.Selected.

title: Default selected items for modern combo boxes
shortDescription: Set edit-form defaults for modern combo boxes without blank selections.
fullDescription: Modern combo boxes need default values that match their Items schema, especially for people, choice, and lookup fields.
problemStatement: Combo boxes appear blank in edit mode when DefaultSelectedItems does not match the Items record shape.
whenNotToUse: Classic dropdown controls with simple Default values
formulaOrCode:
DefaultSelectedItems =
If(
    IsBlank(selectedRequest.AssignedTo),
    Blank(),
    [selectedRequest.AssignedTo]
)
stepByStepInstructions: Inspect the Items schema.
Return a one-row table for single-select combo boxes.
Use Blank() when there is no saved value.
Keep the default shape aligned with the patched shape.
commonMistakes: Returning a record instead of a table.
Using email text as the default for a people picker object.
troubleshooting: If the combo box remains blank, compare the selected record fields to the Items fields.

title: Role-based button visibility using a SharePoint security list
shortDescription: Control app actions from a simple SharePoint role list.
fullDescription: Use a SharePoint list to map users to roles, then drive button visibility from the current user's role.
problemStatement: Hard-coded email checks are brittle and require app edits whenever business roles change.
whenNotToUse: Highly sensitive security boundaries
Apps needing enterprise authorization policies
stepByStepInstructions: Create an AppSecurity list with Email and Role columns.
Load the current user's role on app start.
Use role checks for visibility and behavior.
Keep SharePoint item permissions aligned with the UI intent.
commonMistakes: Treating visibility as true security.
Forgetting users can still access data if list permissions allow it.
troubleshooting: If a valid user sees no buttons, normalize email casing and confirm their security list item is active.

title: Avoid delegation warnings with SharePoint filters
shortDescription: Use SharePoint-friendly filters that keep large lists usable.
fullDescription: A practical filtering approach that avoids common non-delegable formulas and keeps results accurate as lists grow.
problemStatement: Non-delegable formulas can silently return incomplete results once a SharePoint list grows beyond the app limit.
whenNotToUse: Small one-off admin lists
Complex search requiring a real search service
formulaOrCode:
Filter(
    Requests,
    Status.Value = ddStatus.Selected.Value &&
    StartsWith(Title, txtSearch.Value)
)
stepByStepInstructions: Filter first by indexed columns where possible.
Use StartsWith for text search instead of in.
Avoid wrapping SharePoint columns in functions inside Filter.
Test with delegation warnings visible.
commonMistakes: Using Search() on SharePoint lists.
Using Lower(Column) inside the filter.
Ignoring yellow warning triangles.
troubleshooting: If results seem incomplete, simplify filters until warnings disappear, then add filters back one at a time.

title: Patch Request Status as a choice field
shortDescription: Save request lifecycle status updates cleanly from buttons.
fullDescription: A focused variant of choice patching for request workflows where status buttons move items through a lifecycle.
problemStatement: Status buttons often fail when they patch text to a SharePoint choice column.
whenNotToUse: Free-text status fields
Multi-status scenarios
stepByStepInstructions: Confirm the target status exists in SharePoint choices.
Patch the Value object from the action button.
Notify the user after save.
Refresh the selected record.
commonMistakes: Using a status label that does not exist in SharePoint.
Forgetting business validation before approval.
troubleshooting: If the value does not save, compare the exact choice spelling in SharePoint.

title: Build a modal edit form
shortDescription: Open a focused edit panel without leaving the current screen.
fullDescription: Use variables and form modes to create a modal edit experience for quick request or checklist updates.
problemStatement: Users lose context when every small edit requires navigating to a separate screen.
whenNotToUse: Long multi-section forms
Processes requiring a guided wizard
stepByStepInstructions: Add a container overlay.
Drive its Visible property from varShowEditModal.
Set the form Item to varEditingRequest.
Close the modal on cancel or successful save.
commonMistakes: Not resetting the form when the modal closes.
Allowing the background gallery to change the selected item while editing.
troubleshooting: If the wrong item opens, store ThisItem in a variable before showing the modal.

title: Calculate totals from a gallery collection
shortDescription: Summarize editable gallery rows without waiting for a SharePoint save.
fullDescription: Calculate totals from local collection rows to give users immediate feedback in estimate, checklist, or budget screens.
problemStatement: Users need live totals while editing rows before those rows are saved to SharePoint.
whenNotToUse: Authoritative finance calculations needing server-side control
formulaOrCode: Sum(colEstimateRows, Quantity * UnitCost)
stepByStepInstructions: Store gallery rows in a collection.
Update the collection as users edit row controls.
Use Sum, CountRows, or Average on the collection.
Format the result for display.
commonMistakes: Summing text inputs without converting to numbers.
Calculating from gallery controls instead of the collection.
troubleshooting: If totals do not update, confirm Patch(colEstimateRows, ThisItem, ...) fires on control change.

title: Patch a collection of checklist items
shortDescription: Save checklist rows from Power Apps to a SharePoint child list.
fullDescription: Use ForAll and Patch to save multiple checklist rows related to a parent request or project item.
problemStatement: Checklist galleries often need to save several edited rows while preserving their parent relationship.
whenNotToUse: Small temporary rows better stored as JSON
High-volume transactional updates
stepByStepInstructions: Keep the parent record ID available.
Track existing child row IDs when editing.
Patch existing rows or Defaults for new rows.
Reload the child list after save.
commonMistakes: Losing child IDs and creating duplicates.
Saving child rows before the parent request exists.
troubleshooting: If duplicate rows appear, inspect whether collection rows include the SharePoint ID.

title: Recalculate deliverable completion from checklist rows
shortDescription: Roll checklist completion into a parent deliverable percentage.
fullDescription: Calculate completion percentage from child checklist rows and save it back to the parent deliverable.
problemStatement: Parent deliverable status becomes unreliable when checklist progress is manually maintained.
whenNotToUse: Subjective status assessments without checklist criteria
stepByStepInstructions: Load checklist rows for the deliverable.
Count total and completed rows.
Guard against divide-by-zero.
Patch the calculated percentage to the parent.
commonMistakes: Calculating from stale rows.
Not handling empty checklists.
troubleshooting: If completion looks wrong, refresh child rows before recalculation.

title: Format a SharePoint hyperlink field in list JSON
shortDescription: Make hyperlink columns easier to scan and click in SharePoint lists.
fullDescription: Apply list column formatting to render a clean link label for URL fields in SharePoint.
problemStatement: Raw URLs make SharePoint lists noisy and hard for business users to scan.
whenNotToUse: Fields that need multiple links
Complex formatting better handled in a custom app
stepByStepInstructions: Open the column formatting panel.
Paste the JSON.
Adjust the label text if needed.
Test with blank and populated URLs.
commonMistakes: Using JSON formatting to hide required data quality issues.
Forgetting target _blank for reference links.
troubleshooting: If the link does not open, confirm the field stores a valid URL.

title: Send a Power Automate email with an HTML table
shortDescription: Turn selected SharePoint rows into a readable email table.
fullDescription: Use Create HTML table and a styled email body so status updates are readable outside SharePoint.
problemStatement: Plain item dumps in email are hard to read and often miss the business context.
whenNotToUse: Large reports better sent as attachments
Emails with sensitive data requiring special controls
formulaOrCode:
Open Risks

Please review the current high-priority risks.

@{body('Create_HTML_table')}
stepByStepInstructions: Get the SharePoint items.
Select only the columns needed for the email.
Create an HTML table from the selected array.
Place the table inside a short HTML email body.
commonMistakes: Sending too many columns.
Not enabling HTML email body rendering.
troubleshooting: If tags show as text, make sure the email action body is using HTML content.

title: Attach SharePoint list attachments to an email in Power Automate
shortDescription: Collect list item attachments and send them in an email.
fullDescription: Use attachment metadata and content actions to build the array required by the email connector.
problemStatement: SharePoint attachment metadata alone does not include the file content needed by email actions.
whenNotToUse: Large files that should be linked instead
Highly sensitive files needing restricted sharing
stepByStepInstructions: Get attachments for the list item.
Loop through each attachment.
Get attachment content for each file.
Append Name and ContentBytes to an array.
Pass the array to the email Attachments field.
commonMistakes: Using attachment metadata instead of content.
Forgetting to initialize the attachment array.
troubleshooting: If attachments arrive corrupted, confirm ContentBytes is the raw output from Get attachment content.

title: Use OData filters correctly with SharePoint text fields
shortDescription: Filter SharePoint Get items actions with predictable OData expressions.
fullDescription: Use clear OData syntax for text matching in Power Automate SharePoint actions.
problemStatement: Small syntax issues in OData filters can return no rows or cause flow failures.
whenToUse: Find rows by title or key
whenNotToUse: Complex fuzzy searching
Queries needing joins across lists
formulaOrCode: Status eq 'Open' and startswith(Title, 'REQ-')
stepByStepInstructions: Use internal column names.
Wrap text values in single quotes.
Escape single quotes in dynamic values.
Test the filter on a small list first.
commonMistakes: Using display names with spaces instead of internal names.
Forgetting quotes around text values.
troubleshooting: If the action fails, copy the internal field name from list settings and retry.

title: Build a PMO gate approval lifecycle
shortDescription: Model project intake gates with statuses, approvals, and clear ownership.
fullDescription: A practical SharePoint and Power Platform pattern for moving PMO requests through intake, review, approval, and launch readiness gates.
problemStatement: PMO intake processes become hard to manage when status, approval evidence, and decision ownership are not modeled explicitly.
whenNotToUse: Informal teams with no defined approval model
Regulated workflows needing formal records management design
formulaOrCode:
Suggested statuses:
Draft -> Submitted -> Intake Review -> More Info Needed -> Approved for Planning -> Rejected -> Launched
stepByStepInstructions: Define the status lifecycle before building screens.
Create explicit decision fields for approver, date, and comments.
Restrict status buttons by role.
Use Power Automate for notification and audit emails.
Report on aging requests by status.
commonMistakes: Letting users jump to any status from any state.
Mixing workflow status with project health status.
Skipping decision history.
troubleshooting: If the process feels confusing, separate intake status, approval decision, and project health into different fields.

title: Patch a SharePoint multi-choice field
shortDescription: Save several selected choices from a combo box into a SharePoint multi-choice column.
fullDescription: A Power Apps pattern for shaping selected combo box values into the table of Value records expected by SharePoint multi-choice fields.
problemStatement: Multi-choice columns fail or save blank when the app patches a comma-separated text string instead of a table of choice records.
whenNotToUse: High-volume tagging that should use a normalized child list
Plain text keyword storage
stepByStepInstructions: Bind the combo box to Choices(Requests.ImpactAreas).
Allow multiple selection on the control.
Patch a table of records with a Value property.
Reload the item after save to confirm SharePoint accepted the values.
commonMistakes: Joining selected values into one text string.
Using Result when the control returns Value.
Forgetting that multi-choice fields return a table.
troubleshooting: If only one value saves, check whether the combo box is configured for single select.
If values save blank, inspect the selected item property names.

title: Patch a SharePoint multi-person field
shortDescription: Save multiple users from Power Apps into a SharePoint person column.
fullDescription: Use ForAll to build the expanded user records required for a SharePoint person column that allows multiple selections.
problemStatement: Multi-person SharePoint fields require a table of expanded user objects, not a list of emails or display names.
whenNotToUse: Security trimming that should be handled with Microsoft 365 groups
Large audience fields
stepByStepInstructions: Confirm the SharePoint person column allows multiple selections.
Use a user source that returns Mail and DisplayName.
Transform each selected user into an expanded user record.
Patch the resulting table to the person field.
commonMistakes: Reusing the single-person patch object without wrapping it in a table.
Using email addresses without claims.
Not clearing optional Department or JobTitle fields.
troubleshooting: If SharePoint rejects the value, check that every selected user has a Mail value.
If defaults do not load, compare the saved table schema to the combo box Items schema.

title: Create a SharePoint item and child tasks from one submit
shortDescription: Save a parent request, then create related task rows using the new parent ID.
fullDescription: A parent-child save pattern for apps that need one main SharePoint item plus several generated child records.
problemStatement: Child rows cannot be reliably created until the parent SharePoint item exists and the app has its new ID.
whenNotToUse: Simple single-list forms
Workflows needing transactional rollback
stepByStepInstructions: Patch the parent item and store the result.
Use the returned parent ID for child rows.
Loop through the task template collection.
Create each child task with the parent ID and starter status.
commonMistakes: Using LastSubmit from the wrong form.
Creating child rows before the parent item returns.
Saving parent title instead of parent ID.
troubleshooting: If child rows are orphaned, inspect the savedRequest variable immediately after parent Patch.
If duplicates appear, ensure the submit button is disabled during save.

title: Soft-delete SharePoint items from Power Apps
shortDescription: Hide records from users without removing the SharePoint item.
fullDescription: Use an IsArchived or DeletedOn field to preserve audit history while keeping active screens clean.
problemStatement: Hard deletes can remove useful audit context and make accidental deletion hard to recover from.
whenNotToUse: Data that must be purged for compliance
Temporary draft records with no audit value
stepByStepInstructions: Add archive fields to the SharePoint list.
Patch archive values instead of calling Remove.
Filter active galleries where IsArchived is not true.
Create an admin view for archived records.
commonMistakes: Calling Remove when the business expects recoverability.
Forgetting to filter archived records out of galleries.
troubleshooting: If archived items still appear, check for blank IsArchived values and filter with IsArchived <> true.

title: Handle Patch errors with IfError
shortDescription: Show useful save feedback when SharePoint rejects a Power Apps patch.
fullDescription: Wrap Patch calls with IfError so users see clear failure messages and the app can avoid pretending a save succeeded.
problemStatement: Apps often show success notifications even when SharePoint rejected the save due to validation or permissions.
whenNotToUse: Purely local collection edits
Simple forms already using OnFailure well
formulaOrCode:
IfError(
    Patch(Requests, selectedRequest, { Title: txtTitle.Value }),
    Notify("The request could not be saved. Please check required fields and try again.", NotificationType.Error),
    Notify("Request saved.", NotificationType.Success)
)
stepByStepInstructions: Wrap the Patch call in IfError.
Show a specific error notification.
Only show success in the success branch.
Consider logging error details for admin troubleshooting later.
commonMistakes: Putting Notify success after IfError so it always runs.
Hiding SharePoint required-field errors behind generic UI.
troubleshooting: If the error branch never runs, confirm the expression inside IfError is the actual failing call.

title: Build a delegation-safe SharePoint date range filter
shortDescription: Filter large SharePoint lists by date without losing records.
fullDescription: Use direct date comparisons against indexed SharePoint date columns to keep dashboard filters delegable.
problemStatement: Wrapping SharePoint date columns in DateValue or Text inside Filter can break delegation and return incomplete results.
whenNotToUse: Tiny lists where delegation is not a concern
Complex reporting better handled in Power BI
formulaOrCode:
Filter(
    Requests,
    DueDate >= dpStart.SelectedDate &&
    DueDate < DateAdd(dpEnd.SelectedDate, 1, TimeUnit.Days)
)
stepByStepInstructions: Use a real SharePoint date column.
Compare the column directly to date picker values.
Use an exclusive end boundary for full-day matching.
Index the date column in SharePoint for larger lists.
commonMistakes: Using Text(DueDate) in the filter.
Filtering with equals when times are stored with the date.
Forgetting the end date boundary.
troubleshooting: If expected records are missing, check whether DueDate includes time values and use a next-day exclusive end filter.

title: Design SharePoint columns with stable internal names
shortDescription: Avoid painful internal-name surprises before building apps and flows.
fullDescription: Create SharePoint columns with clean internal names first, then rename display labels for business readability.
problemStatement: Columns created with spaces or temporary names can leave awkward internal names that make formulas and flow filters harder to maintain.
whenNotToUse: Existing mature lists where renaming would break integrations
formulaOrCode:
Recommended creation order:
1. Create column as ProjectCode
2. Save the column
3. Rename display label to Project Code
stepByStepInstructions: Create columns with simple internal names.
Avoid spaces, punctuation, and temporary labels during creation.
Rename display labels after saving.
Document internal names for app and flow builders.
commonMistakes: Creating a column as Project Code and later discovering Project_x0020_Code.
Renaming a display name and assuming the internal name changed.
troubleshooting: If an OData filter fails, verify the internal name from list settings rather than the display label.

title: SharePoint list schema for project intake
shortDescription: Start a project intake list with fields that support workflow, reporting, and governance.
fullDescription: A practical starter schema for project request intake that works with Power Apps, approvals, and PMO reporting.
problemStatement: Project intake lists become messy when request details, status, decision history, and prioritization are not separated clearly.
whenNotToUse: Full enterprise portfolio management replacement
Highly regulated intake requiring formal records design
formulaOrCode:
Core columns:
ProjectCode, RequestTitle, BusinessOwner, Sponsor, IntakeStatus, Priority, RequestedStartDate, TargetFinishDate, Decision, DecisionDate, DecisionComments
stepByStepInstructions: Separate intake status from delivery health.
Use choice columns for lifecycle values.
Create people fields for owner, sponsor, and reviewer.
Add decision fields for audit context.
Index status and owner fields for dashboards.
commonMistakes: Using one Status field for both approval lifecycle and project health.
Skipping decision comments.
Making every field required before the draft stage.
troubleshooting: If reports are hard to build, check whether lifecycle, priority, and ownership are stored as structured columns.

title: Document library metadata pattern for project files
shortDescription: Use metadata and views so PMO documents do not depend only on folders.
fullDescription: Design project document libraries with a small set of metadata fields that support views, filtering, and lifecycle management.
problemStatement: Folder-only libraries make it difficult to find files by document type, project phase, owner, or approval status.
whenNotToUse: Simple team file drops with no reporting need
Formal records repositories needing retention design
formulaOrCode:
Suggested metadata:
DocumentType, ProjectPhase, DocumentOwner, ApprovalStatus, EffectiveDate, RelatedProjectCode
stepByStepInstructions: Define a short metadata set before migration.
Use choice fields for document type and phase.
Create views by phase, owner, and approval status.
Keep folder structure shallow if folders are still needed.
commonMistakes: Adding too many required metadata fields.
Using free-text document types.
Creating deep folder trees that hide useful metadata.
troubleshooting: If users resist metadata, reduce required fields and prefill values from the project context where possible.

title: SharePoint permissions pattern for request lists
shortDescription: Balance broad collaboration with controlled approval actions.
fullDescription: Use list permissions, views, and app-level role checks together so users can collaborate without accidentally approving or editing restricted fields.
problemStatement: Power Apps visibility rules do not replace SharePoint permissions, but SharePoint permissions alone may not create the right workflow experience.
whenToUse: Lists with contributor and approver roles
whenNotToUse: Highly confidential data requiring item-level security architecture
Apps with external guest access not yet governed
formulaOrCode:
Recommended layers:
1. SharePoint controls who can read and edit the list
2. Power Apps controls which actions are visible
3. Power Automate records approval decisions and notifications
stepByStepInstructions: Define data access separately from button visibility.
Keep approver-only fields protected through process design.
Use role lists for app behavior.
Use SharePoint groups for actual list access.
Review inherited permissions before launch.
commonMistakes: Treating hidden buttons as security.
Giving everyone edit rights to sensitive decision fields.
Breaking inheritance without documenting why.
troubleshooting: If users can still edit fields outside the app, review list forms, grid edit permissions, and SharePoint group membership.

title: Power Automate approval with SharePoint status sync
shortDescription: Keep approval outcomes and request status aligned in SharePoint.
fullDescription: A flow pattern for starting an approval, recording the decision, and updating a SharePoint choice status consistently.
problemStatement: Approval flows become confusing when the approval outcome and SharePoint request status drift apart.
whenNotToUse: Complex multi-stage approval engines
Approvals needing custom delegation rules
formulaOrCode:
Flow outline:
Trigger -> Start and wait for an approval -> Condition on Outcome -> Update item Status, DecisionDate, DecisionBy, DecisionComments
stepByStepInstructions: Start the approval from the SharePoint item context.
Use outcome conditions for Approved and Rejected.
Update both lifecycle status and decision fields.
Send a confirmation email with the decision summary.
commonMistakes: Updating status before the approval completes.
Not storing approver comments.
Using email notification as the only audit trail.
troubleshooting: If status does not update, inspect the approval outcome value and branch condition casing.

title: Parse JSON sent from Power Apps in Power Automate
shortDescription: Send structured rows from Power Apps to a flow and parse them safely.
fullDescription: Use JSON from Power Apps for lightweight row arrays, then parse and loop through the payload in Power Automate.
problemStatement: Flows that receive collection data as text need a schema before they can loop through rows predictably.
whenToUse: Email table generation from app data
whenNotToUse: Large payloads better saved directly to a data source
Data requiring complex validation before submission
stepByStepInstructions: Serialize the Power Apps collection.
Pass the JSON text into the flow.
Use Parse JSON with a sample payload schema.
Loop through parsed rows and create or update records.
commonMistakes: Changing the app collection schema without updating Parse JSON.
Passing display-only control values instead of clean data fields.
troubleshooting: If Parse JSON fails, capture the exact payload from a flow run and regenerate the schema from that sample.

title: Format dates in Power Automate with Compose
shortDescription: Create reusable formatted date values before email or SharePoint updates.
fullDescription: Use Compose actions to make date formatting explicit and easier to reuse across flow steps.
problemStatement: Date expressions scattered directly inside emails and updates are hard to troubleshoot and easy to format inconsistently.
whenNotToUse: Dates that should remain raw ISO values for downstream systems
formulaOrCode: formatDateTime(triggerOutputs()?['body/DueDate'], 'MMM d, yyyy')
stepByStepInstructions: Add a Compose action near the top of the flow.
Put the formatDateTime expression in Compose.
Use the Compose output in emails or updates.
Name the Compose action clearly.
commonMistakes: Formatting dates before checking for blanks.
Using local-looking formats for system integrations.
troubleshooting: If the flow fails on blank dates, wrap the expression in an if empty check.

title: Update SharePoint items without overwriting important fields
shortDescription: Reduce accidental data loss when a flow updates SharePoint items.
fullDescription: Use focused update steps and read-before-write discipline so flows do not overwrite fields that users or apps changed.
problemStatement: SharePoint Update item actions can unintentionally write stale or blank values if required fields are not handled carefully.
whenNotToUse: Simple single-field updates available through a purpose-built action or API pattern
formulaOrCode:
Flow discipline:
Get item -> calculate only changed fields -> Update item with existing values preserved for required fields
stepByStepInstructions: Get the current item before updating.
Only change fields the flow owns.
Pass existing required field values when the connector requires them.
Avoid updating broad records from stale trigger data.
commonMistakes: Leaving required fields blank in Update item.
Assuming trigger data is still current after approval waits.
Letting one flow own too many unrelated fields.
troubleshooting: If fields revert unexpectedly, compare the flow run inputs to the item version history.

title: Use Try/Catch scopes for Power Automate error handling
shortDescription: Group flow actions into success and failure paths that are easier to support.
fullDescription: Organize Power Automate flows with Try, Catch, and Finally scopes so failures produce clear notifications and cleanup behavior.
problemStatement: Flows with long linear action chains are difficult to troubleshoot when one middle step fails.
whenNotToUse: Tiny personal productivity flows where overhead is not worth it
formulaOrCode:
Pattern:
Scope - Try
Scope - Catch: run after Try has failed, timed out, or skipped
Scope - Finally: run after Try and Catch complete
stepByStepInstructions: Put main work inside a Try scope.
Configure Catch to run after Try failure.
Send a support-friendly failure notification.
Use Finally for cleanup or run logging.
commonMistakes: Only handling one failed action instead of the whole business operation.
Sending failure emails without the item link or flow run link.
troubleshooting: If Catch never runs, inspect the run-after settings on the Catch scope.

title: PMO status report health rollup
shortDescription: Turn scope, schedule, budget, and risk ratings into a clear overall status.
fullDescription: A practical rollup pattern for project status reporting that keeps yellow and red signals visible instead of averaged away.
problemStatement: Overall project status loses credibility when serious schedule or budget risks are buried under a green average.
whenNotToUse: Projects without agreed health definitions
Teams using fully qualitative narratives only
formulaOrCode:
Rollup rule:
If any dimension is Red, overall cannot be Green.
If two or more dimensions are Yellow, overall should be Yellow unless leadership accepts the risk.
stepByStepInstructions: Define health dimensions.
Document what Green, Yellow, and Red mean.
Apply conservative rollup rules.
Require narrative explanation for any Yellow or Red dimension.
commonMistakes: Averaging colors numerically.
Letting teams mark overall green while schedule is red.
Skipping explanation for yellow status.
troubleshooting: If status debates repeat, publish explicit criteria for each color and dimension.

title: Risk, issue, and action log schema
shortDescription: Separate risks, issues, actions, and decisions without making PMs maintain four disconnected tools.
fullDescription: A PMO-friendly schema pattern for tracking risks, issues, actions, and decisions with enough structure for reporting.
problemStatement: Teams blur risks, issues, actions, and decisions together, making executive reporting and follow-up unclear.
whenNotToUse: Very small projects with no formal governance
Enterprise tools already enforcing RAID structure
formulaOrCode:
Core fields:
Type, Title, Description, Owner, DueDate, Impact, Probability, Severity, Status, EscalationNeeded, RelatedProject
stepByStepInstructions: Use a Type field for Risk, Issue, Action, or Decision.
Require owner and status.
Use due dates for actions and decisions.
Use impact and probability only where they make sense.
Create views by project and escalation need.
commonMistakes: Making risk probability required for action items.
Tracking decisions without decision date or decision maker.
Letting every item sit in Open status.
troubleshooting: If reports are noisy, split views by Type and show only fields relevant to that type.

title: Convert rough project notes into an executive summary
shortDescription: Structure messy status notes into a short leadership-ready update.
fullDescription: A reusable writing pattern for turning accomplishments, next steps, risks, and decisions into a concise executive summary.
problemStatement: Status updates often bury the real message in task-level notes that executives do not have time to parse.
whenNotToUse: Detailed project team work logs
Formal incident postmortems
formulaOrCode:
Summary structure:
1. Current status and reason
2. Meaningful progress this period
3. Risks or decisions needing attention
4. Next checkpoint
stepByStepInstructions: Start with the overall status.
Mention only business-relevant progress.
Call out yellow or red risks directly.
End with the next decision or checkpoint.
commonMistakes: Listing every task completed.
Using vague phrases like on track without evidence.
Hiding decisions needed at the bottom.
troubleshooting: If the summary feels too long, remove details that do not change a leader's decision or awareness.

title: Decision log pattern for SharePoint
shortDescription: Capture who decided what, when, and why so decisions do not disappear into meeting notes.
fullDescription: A simple SharePoint decision log pattern that supports PMO reporting and future audit questions without creating heavy process.
problemStatement: Important project decisions are often buried in meeting notes and become hard to trace later.
whenNotToUse: Informal team notes where no formal decision record is needed
formulaOrCode:
Suggested fields:
DecisionTitle, DecisionDescription, DecisionMaker, DecisionDate, Context, Impact, RelatedProject, FollowUpAction
stepByStepInstructions: Create a dedicated decision log list.
Capture decision maker and date as structured fields.
Link decisions to projects or requests.
Review open follow-up actions weekly.
commonMistakes: Writing decisions only as meeting note paragraphs.
Forgetting the decision maker.
Not linking follow-up actions.
troubleshooting: If users avoid the log, add decisions during meeting closeout instead of after the fact.

title: Control Power Apps form mode for new, edit, and view screens
shortDescription: Make form behavior predictable across create, edit, and read-only experiences.
fullDescription: Use NewForm, EditForm, ViewForm, and screen variables deliberately so users do not accidentally edit or overwrite records.
problemStatement: Forms can open in the wrong mode when navigation, selected records, and reset behavior are not handled consistently.
whenNotToUse: Fully custom patch screens without EditForm controls
formulaOrCode:
New button:
NewForm(frmRequest); Navigate(scrRequestForm)

Edit button:
Set(selectedRequest, ThisItem); EditForm(frmRequest); Navigate(scrRequestForm)

View button:
Set(selectedRequest, ThisItem); ViewForm(frmRequest); Navigate(scrRequestForm)
stepByStepInstructions: Set the selected record before navigating for edit or view.
Call the correct form mode function.
Reset forms after successful save or cancel.
Use DisplayMode to keep view-only controls read-only.
commonMistakes: Using EditForm for new records.
Navigating before selectedRequest is set.
Forgetting ResetForm on cancel.
troubleshooting: If the wrong record opens, check whether the form Item property points to selectedRequest or Gallery.Selected.

title: Save draft and submit later in Power Apps
shortDescription: Let users save incomplete requests without triggering the formal workflow.
fullDescription: Use separate Draft and Submitted statuses so business users can save work in progress before routing a request for review.
problemStatement: Users often need to save partial forms, but workflows should not start until the request is intentionally submitted.
whenNotToUse: Very short forms with all required information available immediately
stepByStepInstructions: Add Draft and Submitted choices to the status column.
Use a save draft button that relaxes business validation.
Use a submit button that enforces required fields.
Trigger flows only when status changes to Submitted.
commonMistakes: Starting approval flows on every item creation.
Making all final required fields mandatory for draft saves.
troubleshooting: If draft items start routing, check the flow trigger condition and status value.

title: Disable submit buttons while saving
shortDescription: Prevent duplicate SharePoint items from double-clicked submit buttons.
fullDescription: Use a saving variable to block duplicate submits while Patch or SubmitForm is in progress.
problemStatement: Fast double-clicks can create duplicate records or run approval logic twice.
whenNotToUse: Read-only actions
Pure navigation buttons
stepByStepInstructions: Set varSaving before the save begins.
Disable the button when varSaving is true.
Reset varSaving after success or failure.
Show clear feedback so users know the save is running.
commonMistakes: Resetting varSaving only in the success path.
Leaving the button enabled while a flow call runs.
troubleshooting: If duplicates still appear, confirm every submit path checks the same saving variable.

title: Inline edit and save gallery rows
shortDescription: Let users edit small row fields directly in a gallery and save changes predictably.
fullDescription: Use a local collection for edited gallery rows, then patch only changed values back to SharePoint.
problemStatement: Editing directly against gallery controls can become unreliable when users change several rows before saving.
whenNotToUse: Large editable grids
Financial data requiring strict validation
stepByStepInstructions: Load rows into an editable collection.
Patch the collection as users change controls.
Mark changed rows with IsDirty.
Patch only dirty rows back to SharePoint.
commonMistakes: Reading values directly from gallery controls during ForAll.
Saving every row even when only one changed.
troubleshooting: If changes save to the wrong row, confirm every collection row includes the SharePoint ID.

title: Bulk update selected gallery items
shortDescription: Apply the same status or owner update to several selected SharePoint rows.
fullDescription: Track selected rows in a collection and loop through them when the user confirms a bulk action.
problemStatement: Users waste time opening records one at a time when a shared update applies to several items.
whenNotToUse: Updates requiring unique validation per row
Large batches better handled by Power Automate
stepByStepInstructions: Add a checkbox to each gallery row.
Collect or remove rows as users check items.
Preview the selected count.
Patch selected rows after confirmation.
commonMistakes: Bulk updating without a confirmation step.
Not clearing selected rows after the action completes.
troubleshooting: If some rows fail, add IfError around each Patch and collect failed IDs.

title: Search SharePoint with multiple fields in Power Apps
shortDescription: Build a practical multi-field search while staying honest about delegation limits.
fullDescription: Combine delegable StartsWith filters for key fields and avoid non-delegable broad text scans on large SharePoint lists.
problemStatement: Users expect search across several fields, but SharePoint delegation limits make broad contains searches risky.
whenNotToUse: True full-text search requirements
Very large data sets needing Microsoft Search or Dataverse
formulaOrCode:
Filter(
    Requests,
    IsBlank(txtSearch.Value) ||
    StartsWith(Title, txtSearch.Value) ||
    StartsWith(ProjectCode, txtSearch.Value)
)
stepByStepInstructions: Choose a small number of searchable fields.
Prefer StartsWith over in for SharePoint.
Index high-use columns.
Tell users which fields search covers.
commonMistakes: Using Search() against a large SharePoint list.
Searching every column with non-delegable formulas.
troubleshooting: If results are incomplete, simplify the formula until delegation warnings disappear.

title: Cascading dropdowns from SharePoint lists
shortDescription: Filter child dropdown options based on a parent selection.
fullDescription: Use parent-child SharePoint reference data to guide users through valid dropdown combinations.
problemStatement: Users can pick invalid combinations when every dropdown shows all possible values.
whenNotToUse: Small flat option sets
Reference data that changes constantly without governance
formulaOrCode:
Filter(
    ServiceCatalog,
    DepartmentId = cmbDepartment.Selected.ID
)
stepByStepInstructions: Create a reference list with parent IDs.
Set the child control Items to a filtered source.
Reset the child control when the parent changes.
Patch both selected values or lookup IDs as needed.
commonMistakes: Not resetting the child dropdown when parent changes.
Filtering on display names instead of stable IDs.
troubleshooting: If child options do not appear, inspect whether the parent selected record has ID or Id.

title: Cache the current user's profile in Power Apps
shortDescription: Load user profile details once and reuse them across the app.
fullDescription: Store the current user's profile and role context in variables to reduce repeated connector calls and simplify formulas.
problemStatement: Repeated profile lookups make formulas noisy and can slow screens that use the same user details in many places.
whenToUse: Request forms that default requester fields
whenNotToUse: Apps that do not use profile data
Scenarios requiring real-time manager lookup on every action
formulaOrCode:
Set(currentUserEmail, Lower(User().Email));
Set(currentUserProfile, Office365Users.MyProfileV2());
stepByStepInstructions: Set current user variables when the app starts.
Normalize email casing.
Reuse the variables in filters and defaults.
Refresh only when the user explicitly needs updated profile data.
commonMistakes: Calling Office365Users.MyProfileV2 in many control properties.
Comparing mixed-case email strings.
troubleshooting: If role lookups fail, compare Lower(User().Email) to the stored email in the security list.

title: Show a useful empty state for galleries
shortDescription: Give users clear feedback when filters return no rows.
fullDescription: Use a visible empty-state container to explain whether a gallery has no records, no matches, or needs a filter change.
problemStatement: Blank gallery screens make users wonder whether the app is broken, loading, or simply has no matching records.
whenNotToUse: Screens where empty content is impossible by design
formulaOrCode: conEmptyState.Visible = CountRows(galRequests.AllItems) = 0
stepByStepInstructions: Add an empty-state container near the gallery.
Show it when the gallery has no items.
Use message text that reflects the current filter state.
Offer a reset filters button where useful.
commonMistakes: Showing a generic no data message while data is still loading.
Not distinguishing no records from no search matches.
troubleshooting: If the empty state flickers, add a loading variable and hide the empty state while data loads.

title: Optimistically update a local collection after Patch
shortDescription: Make saved changes appear immediately while SharePoint refresh catches up.
fullDescription: Patch SharePoint and the local collection together so dashboards feel responsive without waiting for a full refresh.
problemStatement: Refreshing a large SharePoint list after every save can make apps feel slow and disrupt the current screen.
whenNotToUse: High-conflict records requiring fresh server state
Complex calculated SharePoint fields
formulaOrCode:
Set(savedItem, Patch(Requests, selectedRequest, { RequestStatus: { Value: "Closed" } }));
Patch(colRequests, LookUp(colRequests, ID = savedItem.ID), savedItem);
stepByStepInstructions: Store the result of Patch.
Patch the matching row in the local collection.
Refresh in the background only when needed.
Fall back to a full reload after errors.
commonMistakes: Updating the collection before confirming Patch succeeded.
Forgetting SharePoint calculated fields may need a refresh.
troubleshooting: If the collection shows stale calculated values, reload the saved item by ID after Patch.

title: Index SharePoint columns for large list views
shortDescription: Design list views and filters that keep working past the list view threshold.
fullDescription: Index high-use columns and align views with the way Power Apps, Power Automate, and business users filter the list.
problemStatement: Lists that work during testing can become unreliable when item counts grow and filters are not supported by indexes.
whenNotToUse: Small lookup lists with only a few dozen rows
formulaOrCode:
Good index candidates:
Status, AssignedTo, ProjectCode, DueDate, IsArchived, Created
stepByStepInstructions: Identify common filters before launch.
Index status, owner, date, and archive fields.
Create views that filter on indexed columns first.
Avoid broad unfiltered all-items views for large lists.
commonMistakes: Waiting until after threshold problems appear.
Indexing many unused columns instead of the key filters.
troubleshooting: If a view fails, simplify it to one indexed filter first, then add secondary conditions.

title: Govern SharePoint choice values before apps depend on them
shortDescription: Keep choice columns stable enough for formulas, filters, and reporting.
fullDescription: Treat high-value SharePoint choices as governed reference data so app logic and reporting do not break when labels change.
problemStatement: Uncontrolled choice edits can break formulas, flow conditions, dashboard filters, and historical reporting.
whenNotToUse: Ad hoc notes
Fields intentionally meant for open-ended text
formulaOrCode:
Governed choices example:
Draft, Submitted, Intake Review, More Info Needed, Approved, Rejected, Closed
stepByStepInstructions: Define choices before building automations.
Document which choices drive business logic.
Restrict who can edit list settings.
Use migration steps when labels must change.
commonMistakes: Letting makers rename status choices casually.
Using different labels for the same lifecycle across lists.
troubleshooting: If a flow branch stops working, compare the exact current choice label to the condition value.

title: Format SharePoint status values as color-coded pills
shortDescription: Make status columns easier to scan in SharePoint lists.
fullDescription: Use column formatting JSON to show request or project statuses as compact color-coded labels.
problemStatement: Plain text status columns make busy SharePoint views harder to scan.
whenNotToUse: Lists where color meaning has not been agreed
Accessibility-sensitive views without text labels
stepByStepInstructions: Open column formatting for the status column.
Paste the JSON.
Adjust colors to match your status labels.
Keep the text visible inside the pill.
commonMistakes: Using color without the status text.
Formatting labels that do not match the actual choice values.
troubleshooting: If colors do not apply, compare the current field text to the condition labels.

title: Choose between SharePoint calculated columns and Power Apps formulas
shortDescription: Decide where calculations should live before users depend on the output.
fullDescription: Use SharePoint calculated columns for simple list-visible calculations and Power Apps formulas for interactive screen behavior.
problemStatement: Putting calculations in the wrong layer can create stale values, confusing views, or formulas that are hard to maintain.
whenNotToUse: Complex business rules
Values requiring external data
Interactive calculations before save
formulaOrCode:
Rule of thumb:
SharePoint calculated column: simple saved display logic
Power Apps formula: interactive UI logic
Power Automate: cross-record or process-driven updates
stepByStepInstructions: Decide who needs the calculated value and where.
Use SharePoint for simple persisted display values.
Use Power Apps for live screen calculations.
Use flow when the value depends on process events.
commonMistakes: Using calculated columns for business rules that need approvals.
Expecting SharePoint calculations to behave like app variables.
troubleshooting: If a calculated value appears stale in Power Apps, refresh and reload the saved record by ID.

title: Choose lookup columns vs choice columns
shortDescription: Pick the right SharePoint field type for stable app and reporting behavior.
fullDescription: Use choice columns for small governed value sets and lookup columns when values need their own metadata or lifecycle.
problemStatement: Choosing a field type casually can make future filtering, reporting, and maintenance harder.
whenNotToUse: Free text descriptions
High-volume many-to-many relationships
formulaOrCode:
Use Choice when values are few and simple.
Use Lookup when values need owners, active flags, descriptions, or reporting metadata.
stepByStepInstructions: List the values and how often they change.
Check whether each value needs extra fields.
Use choice for simple stable lists.
Use lookup for governed reference records.
commonMistakes: Using choices for complex reference data.
Using lookups where a simple choice would be easier for makers.
troubleshooting: If formulas are becoming complex around a choice value, consider moving that concept to a reference list.

title: SharePoint site navigation for business apps
shortDescription: Organize list, page, and app links so users can find the working surface quickly.
fullDescription: A lightweight navigation pattern for SharePoint sites that host Power Apps, lists, dashboards, and guidance pages.
problemStatement: Business app sites become confusing when users see raw list links, old pages, and admin-only assets in the same navigation.
whenNotToUse: Single-purpose list pages with only one audience
formulaOrCode:
Suggested navigation:
Home, Submit Request, My Requests, Team Dashboard, Reports, Help
stepByStepInstructions: Start navigation with user tasks, not backend lists.
Group admin links away from daily user links.
Use clear labels that match the business process.
Review navigation after launch based on support questions.
commonMistakes: Putting every list in top navigation.
Using maker names instead of business task names.
troubleshooting: If users bookmark list views directly, add clearer task links on the site home page.

title: SharePoint list release checklist
shortDescription: Review schema, views, permissions, and app dependencies before launch.
fullDescription: A practical checklist for releasing SharePoint-backed apps without missing common governance and support details.
problemStatement: Small SharePoint solutions often launch before permissions, indexes, views, and ownership are ready.
whenNotToUse: Personal prototype lists that are not yet shared
formulaOrCode:
Release checks:
Schema reviewed, internal names documented, permissions verified, indexes added, views tested, flows owned by service account or team owner, support owner assigned
stepByStepInstructions: Review field names and required settings.
Verify permissions with a non-owner test user.
Check indexed filters and default views.
Confirm flow ownership and failure notifications.
Document support contacts.
commonMistakes: Testing only as site owner.
Forgetting flow ownership transfer.
Launching with all-items views only.
troubleshooting: If users report missing data, test the exact SharePoint group membership and view filters they have.

title: Run a flow only when status changes
shortDescription: Use trigger conditions so flows do not run on every SharePoint edit.
fullDescription: Reduce noisy flow runs by checking status and previous values before starting approval or notification logic.
problemStatement: Flows that trigger on every item update can send duplicate emails, start repeated approvals, or waste run capacity.
whenNotToUse: Flows that intentionally audit every item change
formulaOrCode: @equals(triggerOutputs()?['body/RequestStatus/Value'], 'Submitted')
stepByStepInstructions: Identify the exact status that should start the flow.
Add a trigger condition to the SharePoint trigger.
Use status fields instead of free-text flags.
Test edits that should and should not start the flow.
commonMistakes: Putting the condition after the trigger, which still consumes a run.
Using the display label incorrectly for choice fields.
troubleshooting: If the flow never starts, inspect the trigger payload and confirm the choice value path.

title: Start an approval from a Power Apps button
shortDescription: Let users intentionally launch an approval flow from inside the app.
fullDescription: Call a Power Automate flow from Power Apps after validating the record and passing the SharePoint item ID.
problemStatement: Approval flows need a reliable item ID and should start only after users intentionally submit a valid request.
whenNotToUse: Simple auto-notifications
Processes that should start from every list item creation
formulaOrCode:
Set(savedRequest, Patch(Requests, selectedRequest, { RequestStatus: { Value: "Submitted" } }));
StartApprovalFlow.Run(savedRequest.ID);
stepByStepInstructions: Patch and validate the SharePoint item first.
Store the saved item result.
Call the flow with the saved item ID.
Have the flow get the item by ID before starting approval.
commonMistakes: Calling the flow before the item save completes.
Passing title instead of item ID.
Starting approval without locking the request status.
troubleshooting: If the flow cannot find the item, log the ID passed from Power Apps and compare it to SharePoint.

title: Add a button-style link to Power Automate emails
shortDescription: Send clean action emails with a clear link back to the SharePoint item or app.
fullDescription: Use simple HTML in email bodies to create a visually clear call-to-action button for reviewers and stakeholders.
problemStatement: Plain URLs in workflow emails are easy to miss and make action requests feel less polished.
whenNotToUse: Environments where email clients strip HTML heavily
formulaOrCode: Open request
stepByStepInstructions: Build or retrieve the item link.
Add a short instruction sentence.
Place the styled anchor after the summary.
Test the email in Outlook desktop and web.
commonMistakes: Sending a button with no plain text context.
Using dark text on dark button backgrounds.
troubleshooting: If the button does not render, confirm the email body is HTML and not plain text.

title: Route approval to a user's manager
shortDescription: Use Microsoft 365 profile data to send approval requests to the requester's manager.
fullDescription: Look up the requester's manager in Power Automate and use that person as the approval assignee.
problemStatement: Manual approver selection creates routing mistakes when approval should follow the organizational reporting line.
whenNotToUse: Matrix organizations where manager is not the correct approver
Processes with delegated approval rules
formulaOrCode:
Flow outline:
Get item -> Get manager (V2) for Requester Email -> Start and wait for an approval assigned to manager mail
stepByStepInstructions: Store requester email in SharePoint.
Use Get manager V2 with that email.
Handle cases where no manager is returned.
Start the approval assigned to the manager's mail.
commonMistakes: Assuming every user has a manager in the directory.
Using display name instead of mail for approval assignment.
troubleshooting: If manager lookup fails, test the same user in the Office 365 Users connector and add a fallback approver.

title: Send reminders before a SharePoint due date
shortDescription: Notify owners about upcoming due dates without sending reminders for closed items.
fullDescription: Use a scheduled flow with an OData filter to find open items due soon and send targeted reminders.
problemStatement: Manual follow-up is unreliable when task owners need reminders before due dates.
whenNotToUse: High-volume reminder systems needing notification preferences and throttling
formulaOrCode: Status ne 'Closed' and DueDate le '@{formatDateTime(addDays(utcNow(), 3), 'yyyy-MM-dd')}'
stepByStepInstructions: Create a scheduled flow.
Get open items due within the reminder window.
Send one concise email per owner or item.
Mark reminder sent only if duplicate prevention is needed.
commonMistakes: Sending reminders for closed items.
Using local date formats inside OData filters.
troubleshooting: If no items return, inspect the generated filter string and internal date column name.

title: Create a Planner task from a SharePoint item
shortDescription: Turn approved requests or action items into Planner tasks.
fullDescription: Use a flow to create Planner tasks from SharePoint rows while preserving the source item link.
problemStatement: Teams often approve or capture work in SharePoint but need execution tracked in Planner.
whenNotToUse: Complex project schedules better handled in Project or Jira
formulaOrCode:
Flow outline:
When item status is Approved -> Create Planner task -> Update SharePoint item with Planner task ID and link
stepByStepInstructions: Trigger from approved SharePoint items.
Create a Planner task with title, due date, and owner.
Add the SharePoint item link to task notes.
Write the Planner task ID back to SharePoint.
commonMistakes: Creating duplicate Planner tasks on every edit.
Not storing the Planner task ID for traceability.
troubleshooting: If duplicates appear, check for an existing PlannerTaskId before creating a new task.

title: Document a Power Automate flow inventory
shortDescription: Capture the essential maintenance details for each production flow.
fullDescription: Use a simple inventory structure to track flow purpose, owner, trigger, dependencies, and support notes.
problemStatement: Production flows become risky when nobody knows what they do, who owns them, or what breaks if they stop.
whenNotToUse: Personal experiments that will be deleted soon
formulaOrCode:
Inventory fields:
FlowName, BusinessPurpose, Owner, TriggerType, ConnectedLists, Criticality, FailureNotification, LastReviewedDate, SupportNotes
stepByStepInstructions: Create a flow inventory list.
Document each production flow's trigger and purpose.
Capture ownership and support routing.
Review the inventory quarterly.
commonMistakes: Documenting only the technical trigger.
Skipping ownership and failure notification details.
troubleshooting: If support cannot triage failures, add source list links and business process notes to the inventory.

title: Avoid infinite update loops in SharePoint flows
shortDescription: Stop a flow from retriggering itself after it updates the same item.
fullDescription: Use trigger conditions, status fields, or flow-owned flags to prevent SharePoint update loops.
problemStatement: A flow that updates the item that triggered it can run repeatedly unless the trigger is constrained.
whenNotToUse: Flows that do not update their trigger item
formulaOrCode:
Loop prevention options:
- Trigger only when Status equals Submitted
- Set ProcessedByFlow to true
- Exit when Modified By is the flow owner account
stepByStepInstructions: Identify whether the flow updates the triggering item.
Add trigger conditions or guard fields.
Update the guard field after processing.
Test with multiple edits to the same item.
commonMistakes: Adding a condition inside the flow instead of at the trigger.
Updating the same status value repeatedly.
troubleshooting: If runs keep chaining, inspect run history to see which update action retriggers the flow.

title: PMO intake scoring model
shortDescription: Score requests by value, urgency, effort, risk, and strategic alignment.
fullDescription: Use a transparent scoring model to help PMOs compare incoming requests without pretending the score is the only decision factor.
problemStatement: Project requests are hard to compare when every sponsor describes value, urgency, and effort differently.
whenNotToUse: Emergency work that bypasses normal scoring
Small teams with very low intake volume
formulaOrCode:
Example score:
(Value * 3) + (Urgency * 2) + (StrategicAlignment * 3) - (Effort * 2) - Risk
stepByStepInstructions: Define scoring factors and scales.
Weight factors based on leadership priorities.
Show the score and the underlying answers.
Use score as an input to review, not an automatic decision.
commonMistakes: Hiding the scoring formula.
Treating precision as truth.
Ignoring capacity constraints.
troubleshooting: If scores feel wrong, review weighting with real historical requests and adjust openly.

title: Steering committee decision pack structure
shortDescription: Prepare concise decision materials for executive review.
fullDescription: Structure steering committee updates around decisions needed, options, impacts, recommendation, and timing.
problemStatement: Steering meetings drift when materials describe activity but do not clearly frame the decisions leaders must make.
whenNotToUse: Routine team status meetings with no executive decision needed
formulaOrCode:
Decision pack sections:
Decision needed, context, options, recommendation, impacts, deadline, owner
stepByStepInstructions: State the decision on the first page.
Limit background to what changes the decision.
Compare options clearly.
Name the recommendation and the deadline.
commonMistakes: Presenting status without an ask.
Burying the recommendation after too much history.
troubleshooting: If leaders ask what you need from them, rewrite the first section around the decision statement.

title: Track baseline vs forecast milestone dates
shortDescription: Show schedule movement without rewriting history.
fullDescription: Store baseline, current forecast, and actual milestone dates separately so schedule variance is visible.
problemStatement: Teams lose schedule accountability when milestone dates are overwritten without preserving the original baseline.
whenNotToUse: Tiny efforts with no meaningful baseline
formulaOrCode:
Milestone fields:
BaselineDate, ForecastDate, ActualDate, VarianceDays, VarianceReason
stepByStepInstructions: Capture baseline date at approval.
Update forecast date when the plan changes.
Set actual date when completed.
Report variance between baseline and forecast or actual.
commonMistakes: Overwriting baseline dates.
Reporting only current forecast without variance context.
troubleshooting: If variance is disputed, review when baseline was approved and who changed forecast dates.

title: Define risk escalation thresholds
shortDescription: Make risk escalation consistent across projects.
fullDescription: Use agreed severity, probability, and timing thresholds to decide when risks require sponsor or steering attention.
problemStatement: Risk escalation becomes political or inconsistent when teams do not share clear thresholds.
whenNotToUse: Informal task tracking where escalation is unnecessary
formulaOrCode:
Escalate when:
Impact is High and Probability is Medium or High
or mitigation owner is blocked
or decision is needed within 10 business days
stepByStepInstructions: Define impact and probability scales.
Set escalation rules.
Add an EscalationNeeded field.
Review escalated risks in governance meetings.
commonMistakes: Escalating every risk.
Escalating only after the risk becomes an issue.
Skipping owner and decision needed fields.
troubleshooting: If escalations are noisy, separate watchlist risks from active decision-needed risks.

title: Action item owner and due date discipline
shortDescription: Keep action logs from becoming vague parking lots.
fullDescription: Make action items useful by requiring a single owner, due date, status, and next follow-up note.
problemStatement: Action logs lose value when items have no owner, no due date, or unclear completion criteria.
whenNotToUse: Personal notes that are not shared commitments
formulaOrCode:
Minimum action item fields:
Action, Owner, DueDate, Status, NextUpdate, RelatedDecisionOrRisk
stepByStepInstructions: Write actions as outcomes, not topics.
Assign one accountable owner.
Set a due date before closing the meeting.
Review overdue actions before adding new ones.
commonMistakes: Using a team as the owner.
Leaving due date blank.
Closing actions without evidence or note.
troubleshooting: If actions stay open too long, add aging views and ask owners for revised dates or blockers.

title: Weekly project status email template
shortDescription: Send a concise weekly update that leaders can scan quickly.
fullDescription: Use a consistent email structure for overall status, progress, next steps, risks, decisions, and asks.
problemStatement: Weekly updates vary too much when every PM writes them from scratch.
whenNotToUse: Detailed working-session notes
Formal steering committee packs
formulaOrCode:
Subject: Project status - [Project Name] - [Green/Yellow/Red]

Overall status:
Progress this week:
Next week:
Risks/issues:
Decisions or help needed:
stepByStepInstructions: Put project name and health in the subject.
Lead with overall status.
Keep progress and next steps brief.
Call out decisions or help needed clearly.
commonMistakes: Writing long narrative paragraphs.
Leaving red or yellow status unexplained.
Burying asks at the end.
troubleshooting: If leaders do not respond, move the decision needed line higher and make the ask more specific.

title: Project closeout checklist
shortDescription: Close projects with decisions, documentation, ownership, and lessons learned captured.
fullDescription: A PMO closeout checklist that keeps finished projects from leaving loose ends behind.
problemStatement: Projects are often marked complete before documentation, ownership, support, and lessons learned are actually wrapped up.
whenNotToUse: Tiny tasks with no handoff or durable deliverables
formulaOrCode:
Closeout areas:
Acceptance, open actions, support owner, documentation, access cleanup, lessons learned, archive location
stepByStepInstructions: Confirm sponsor acceptance.
Resolve or transfer open actions.
Document support owner and runbook location.
Capture lessons learned.
Archive project files and decisions.
commonMistakes: Closing while actions are still ownerless.
Skipping support transition.
Saving lessons learned where nobody can find them.
troubleshooting: If closeout stalls, separate must-close items from nice-to-have retrospective improvements.

title: Change request lifecycle
shortDescription: Track project scope changes from request through decision and implementation.
fullDescription: Use structured fields and lifecycle states to keep change requests clear, comparable, and auditable.
problemStatement: Scope changes become confusing when request, impact analysis, decision, and implementation are not tracked separately.
whenNotToUse: Minor backlog grooming changes that do not affect scope, schedule, or budget
formulaOrCode:
Lifecycle:
Draft -> Submitted -> Impact Review -> Decision Needed -> Approved -> Implemented -> Closed
stepByStepInstructions: Capture the requested change and reason.
Document schedule, budget, scope, and risk impact.
Route decision to the right approver.
Record decision and implementation status.
commonMistakes: Approving changes without impact analysis.
Mixing change request status with project health.
Skipping rejected change history.
troubleshooting: If change requests pile up, add a Decision Needed view and review it weekly.

title: Reusable Power Apps screen header pattern
shortDescription: Standardize title, status, back, and save actions across app screens.
fullDescription: Use a consistent header component or container pattern so business apps feel coherent and easier to maintain.
problemStatement: Apps become harder to use when each screen has different navigation and action placement.
whenNotToUse: Single-screen prototypes
Apps with radically different screen purposes
formulaOrCode:
Header inputs:
TitleText, StatusText, ShowBack, ShowSave, OnBack, OnSave
stepByStepInstructions: Define common header needs.
Create a component or repeated container pattern.
Expose title, status, and action inputs.
Use the same placement across screens.
commonMistakes: Putting too many screen-specific controls in the shared header.
Changing button placement from screen to screen.
troubleshooting: If the header becomes hard to reuse, split common navigation from screen-specific commands.

title: Show accessible status text with color badges
shortDescription: Use color as a scan aid without making it the only signal.
fullDescription: Pair status colors with visible text so users can understand status regardless of display settings or color perception.
problemStatement: Color-only status indicators are hard to use and can create accessibility issues.
whenNotToUse: Purely decorative labels with no business meaning
formulaOrCode:
Text = ThisItem.Status.Value
Fill = Switch(ThisItem.Status.Value, "Approved", ColorValue("#DCFCE7"), "Rejected", ColorValue("#FEE2E2"), ColorValue("#E0F2FE"))
stepByStepInstructions: Display the status text inside the badge.
Use color to reinforce meaning.
Keep contrast readable.
Avoid relying on color alone in instructions.
commonMistakes: Showing only a colored dot.
Using similar colors for different states.
troubleshooting: If users misread statuses, add icons or stronger labels in addition to color.

title: Use SharePoint attachments safely in Power Apps
shortDescription: Know when list attachments are enough and when a document library is the better design.
fullDescription: Use SharePoint list attachments for lightweight supporting evidence, but move document-heavy scenarios to document libraries.
problemStatement: List attachments are convenient, but they can become limiting when files need metadata, lifecycle, or document governance.
whenNotToUse: Controlled documents
Files needing metadata
Large document sets or review workflows
formulaOrCode:
Decision rule:
Use list attachments for lightweight evidence.
Use a document library for managed project files.
stepByStepInstructions: Identify whether files need metadata.
Use list attachments only for simple supporting files.
Use document libraries for managed documents.
Link library documents back to the request when needed.
commonMistakes: Using list attachments as a project document repository.
Expecting attachment metadata that SharePoint list attachments do not provide.
troubleshooting: If users ask for file views, approvals, or metadata, move the design to a document library.

title: External sharing review pattern
shortDescription: Review external access before launching a SharePoint-backed business process.
fullDescription: Use a lightweight external sharing checklist to reduce accidental exposure in project, vendor, or client-facing sites.
problemStatement: External sharing settings are often inherited or misunderstood, which can expose content beyond the intended audience.
whenNotToUse: Fully internal sites with no guest access allowed at tenant level
formulaOrCode:
Review checklist:
Site sharing setting, guest users, anonymous links, unique permissions, sensitive libraries, owner group membership
stepByStepInstructions: Check the site sharing policy.
Review guest users and links.
Inspect unique permissions on sensitive libraries.
Confirm site owners understand sharing rules.
Document cleanup actions.
commonMistakes: Reviewing only top-level site members.
Ignoring anonymous or anyone links.
Leaving old guests after project closeout.
troubleshooting: If access looks too broad, review sharing links and broken inheritance before changing group membership.

title: Assign content ownership for SharePoint hub pages
shortDescription: Keep intranet and PMO content fresh by naming owners and review dates.
fullDescription: Use simple ownership metadata for SharePoint pages so important guidance does not quietly go stale.
problemStatement: SharePoint pages lose trust when no one owns updates or review cycles.
whenNotToUse: Temporary pages with a known retirement date
formulaOrCode:
Page metadata:
ContentOwner, ReviewFrequency, LastReviewedDate, NextReviewDate, ContentStatus
stepByStepInstructions: Add ownership metadata to site pages.
Name a business owner for each important page.
Create a review view by next review date.
Archive or refresh stale content.
commonMistakes: Making IT the owner of business process content.
Publishing pages without review dates.
troubleshooting: If pages go stale, create a monthly owner review view and send reminders.

title: Escalate approvals that time out
shortDescription: Handle approvals that sit unanswered for too long.
fullDescription: Use timeout handling or scheduled follow-up flows so approval requests do not disappear into inboxes.
problemStatement: Approval processes stall when approvers do not respond and no escalation path exists.
whenNotToUse: Low-priority FYI reviews
Processes where silence is acceptable
formulaOrCode:
Escalation approach:
Start approval -> wait with timeout or track SubmittedOn -> if overdue, notify backup approver or sponsor
stepByStepInstructions: Define the allowed approval response window.
Store submitted date and current approver.
Detect overdue approvals.
Notify backup approver or sponsor with context.
commonMistakes: Escalating without telling the original approver.
Not storing enough context for the backup approver.
troubleshooting: If escalations fire too early, check time zone assumptions and the submitted timestamp.

title: Send a Teams notification for high-risk items
shortDescription: Notify a channel when a SharePoint risk or issue crosses a threshold.
fullDescription: Use Power Automate to post concise Teams messages for high-priority risks, issues, or blocked actions.
problemStatement: Important risks can sit unnoticed in SharePoint when teams rely only on manual list review.
whenNotToUse: Every low-priority list change
Channels already overloaded with notifications
formulaOrCode:
Flow outline:
When item created or modified -> if Severity is High and Status is Open -> Post Teams message with item link
stepByStepInstructions: Define which statuses and severities should notify Teams.
Trigger from item changes.
Post only concise context and a link.
Avoid repeated messages for unchanged items.
commonMistakes: Posting every update to Teams.
Leaving out the owner or link back to the item.
troubleshooting: If the channel gets noisy, add a NotifiedOn field or trigger only on escalation changes.

title: Benefits realization tracker
shortDescription: Track expected benefits, owners, measurement dates, and realized outcomes after delivery.
fullDescription: A PMO pattern for following benefits beyond project closeout so value claims can be checked against real outcomes.
problemStatement: Projects often claim benefits during approval but stop tracking whether those benefits were realized after launch.
whenNotToUse: Small operational tasks with no measurable benefit claim
formulaOrCode:
Benefit fields:
BenefitDescription, Metric, BaselineValue, TargetValue, MeasurementDate, BenefitOwner, ActualValue, RealizationStatus
stepByStepInstructions: Capture expected benefits during intake.
Name a benefit owner.
Define how and when each benefit will be measured.
Review actual outcomes after launch.
Report variance to leadership.
commonMistakes: Using vague benefits with no metric.
Assigning benefit ownership only to the project manager.
Stopping tracking at project closeout.
troubleshooting: If benefits cannot be measured, rewrite them as observable outcomes or mark them as qualitative.
